items.q: If a car has negative acceleration, what does that necessarily mean?
items.correct: Its acceleration is in the negative direction (often opposite to the chosen positive direction); it may be slowing down or speeding up in reverse.
items.misconception: Negative acceleration always means the car is slowing down.
items.random_wrong: Negative acceleration means the car is moving backwards.
items.why: Students conflate 'deceleration' with 'negative acceleration'; sign indicates direction relative to the chosen axis, not necessarily slowing.

items.q: Was the Big Bang an explosion of matter into pre-existing space?
items.correct: No — it was the expansion of space itself, beginning from a hot dense state; there was no 'outside' for matter to expand into.
items.misconception: Yes — it was a giant explosion at a point, and galaxies are still flying outwards from it.
items.random_wrong: Yes — it was a bomb-like event that destroyed an earlier universe.
items.why: Pop-science imagery of a fiery explosion encourages the wrong mental model; in fact every point in the universe was once 'the centre'.

items.q: On a distance-time graph, a flat horizontal line means.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Stationary. “Moving fast” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: To convert m/s to km/h you.
items.why: Correct answer: Multiply by 3.6. “Divide by 3.6” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 90. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
